author: deme1744
content:
I am not directly working on the BAL implementation, so I do not have a very good insight of the development of BAL tests/specs, but the way this specific issue was discovered (zkevm eest tests hitting it by chance?) raises a concern for me about how systematic the testing is being approached.

More of these ordering or a like issues might still exist. Do we have a view on how much of the code that matters here is covered by the eest bal testing (i.e. for ref implementation / spec)?

author: nero_eth
content:
Testing coverage is quite broad already, for sure e.g. relative to other EIPs, BALs is already among the most tested EIPs, but there's definitely always a need for more eyes.
We've now entered a phase in which more and more people look into the EIP, specs, test coverage etc, which I can tell by the increased engagement with the EIP, multple independent contributors raising points where things can be clarified/reworded/further-specified etc. So I'm quite positive but would still be great if you can take a look too.

author: deme1744
content:
Yes, it does seems a lot of testing is already being done, which I think is especially important for BAL. The risk with BAL is that it indirectly adds a whole set of new rules on state accesses and such that before did not exist (might have existed in EIPs, or not, or ambiguously). Hence, it is not necessarily always clear from just the "new feature" what extra needs to be done.

Of course there is the spec (ref implementation), but most (all?) of these clients were implemented before its existence.

author: raxhvl
content:
Its difficult to predict. I would say most standard paths are covered by tests. 

The issues coming up now are niche edge cases where clients access state out of order.  7702 had most of these - because it loads multiple accounts so the edge cases can be chained between authority and delgate and the surface area increases. I think we should start fuzzing inputs to expose more.

author: raxhvl
content: BAL forces clients to sync up on reads not just writes. This is something new in the protocol, although the tests are in a good shape - there could always be a case where two clients read different state for the same tx (and arrive at the same post state; because how each interpreted the EIP)  - we are constantly discovering such txs and tightnening how clients implement existing EIPs. Fuzzing should help discover more of these.

author: nero_eth
content:
We know all the opcodes that can get items on the BAL, SSTORE, SLOAD, CALL\*, EXT\*, CREATE\*, BALANCE, COINBASE, SELFDESTRUCT, sys contracts, withdrawals, delegations and deployed contract addresses from initcode calls to empty addresses, which should be a complete list(?), and would want tests that eventually enforce the exact same access order we have in the specs, ensuring everything in the specs - every check - is tested to be sure one cannot even have a different order of checks while still passing.

I think we have that but it might be worth double-checking, <@762919002196410400> and <@796570016966770688> , if there is a way how we could test that even stricter, if not done in the most precise way already. I remember, when we had that topic, we created tests particularly for those edge cases, e.g. `test_bal_extcodecopy_and_oog::oog_at_memory_boundary`, but just to double check, might be worth 🙂
